event_blocks.summary: A detailed anatomical heart model is shown as Speaker 1 states that cardiovascular disease and heart failure are the leading causes of death across all demographics, accompanied by subtle electronic background music. The scene shifts to an abstract mechanical device with tubes and connectors, where Speaker 1 poses a hypothetical about replacing a failing heart with a machine. Archival black-and-white footage then depicts a surgical team in an operating room, identified by an 'OPERATING ROOM 1' sign, working with a glass medical device, while Speaker 2 narrates the half-century effort by scientists to create machines mimicking a beating human heart to combat mortality. The visual transitions to a color view of a complex heart-lung machine with rotating parts and tubes, as Speaker 1 discusses the sigmoid adoption curve of technology. In a modern laboratory setting, a man in a lab coat, with 'European Patent Office' text visible, examines a small mechanical component under a magnifying lamp, while Speaker 1 emphasizes recent advancements enabling devices with decade-long lifespans. A man in casual attire is then seen holding a small, intricate mechanical heart model, followed by a robotic arm displaying a similar device, as Speaker 1 concludes that a practical, permanent total artificial heart would represent a major medical accomplishment. The segment concludes with a title card reading 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' and 'Presented By Lilly A MEDICINE COMPANY', adorned with animated pills, as the background music fades.

Heart-Lung Machine.description: A complex mechanical device with rotating parts and tubes, shown in color, likely a heart-lung machine used in open-heart surgery, appearing as Speaker 1 explains the 'sigmoid adoption curve' of technology.

Surgical Team in Operating Room.description: A black-and-white archival clip showing a surgical team in an operating room, with a sign reading 'OPERATING ROOM 1', performing a procedure on a patient under drapes, while Speaker 2 narrates about scientists working to create a heart-mimicking machine.

Mechanical Device (Tubes and Connectors).description: A dimly lit, abstract mechanical apparatus with tubes and connectors, possibly part of a medical machine, shown in a dark environment, as Speaker 1 discusses replacing a bad heart with a machine.

Man in Casual Attire.description: A man in a blue shirt and jeans, holding a small, intricate mechanical heart model, standing in a well-lit, modern workspace, as Speaker 1 discusses the potential of a permanent artificial heart.

Title Card.description: A black background with the text 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' and 'Presented By Lilly A MEDICINE COMPANY', surrounded by animated pills and capsules, appearing as the background music fades out.

Anatomical Heart Model.description: A detailed, colorful model of a human heart with visible arteries and chambers, mounted on a stand, shown in close-up against a dark background, appearing as Speaker 1 introduces the problem of heart failure.

Man in Lab Coat.description: A man wearing a white lab coat and glasses, examining a small mechanical component under a magnifying lamp in a modern laboratory setting, with 'European Patent Office' text visible, as Speaker 1 describes recent technological advancements allowing for long-lasting devices.

Glass Medical Device.description: A transparent glass medical device with tubes, held by a person in a surgical setting, shown in black-and-white archival footage, accompanying Speaker 2's narration about mimicking a human heart.

European Patent Office.description: Text appearing in the top left corner during the lab scene, indicating the source or affiliation of the footage or setting.

Robotic Arm with Mechanical Heart.description: A white robotic arm with a mechanical heart device attached, positioned on a stand in a modern, high-tech environment, also shown as Speaker 1 highlights the significance of a permanent artificial heart.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a detailed anatomical heart model, illuminated against a dark background, as a male voice (Speaker 1) with an authoritative tone explains that "cardiovascular disease and specifically heart failure is the number one cause of death in men and women across just about every demographic." Subtle, electronic background music begins to play. The visual transitions to a dimly lit, abstract mechanical device with tubes and connectors, while Speaker 1 continues, posing a hypothetical about replacing a bad heart with a machine. Black-and-white archival footage then appears, showing a surgical team in an operating room with a 'OPERATING ROOM 1' sign, performing procedures on a patient and handling a glass medical device. During this, a different male voice (Speaker 2) with a narrative, possibly British-sounding accent, describes how "For the last half century, scientists have been working tirelessly to create a machine that can accurately mimic the function of a beating human heart," envisioning devices to combat the world's number one cause of death. The footage shifts to color, displaying a complex heart-lung machine with rotating parts and tubes, as Speaker 1 returns to discuss the "sigmoid adoption curve" of technology. The scene then moves to a modern laboratory where a man in a white lab coat, with 'European Patent Office' text visible, examines a small mechanical component under a magnifying lamp, as Speaker 1 highlights how recent technological advancements allow for devices that could last a decade or more. The video then shows a man in casual attire holding a small, intricate mechanical heart model, followed by a robotic arm with a similar device attached, while Speaker 1 concludes that a practical, permanent total artificial heart would be one of the biggest accomplishments in modern medicine. The segment ends with a title card displaying 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' and 'Presented By Lilly A MEDICINE COMPANY', surrounded by animated pills, as the electronic background music fades out.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins with a title card for 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' and gentle background music, transitioning to archival footage of a surgical team and a narrator explaining the limitations of heart transplants. A modern shot shows a 'Playmate Plus' cooler, followed by graphics illustrating the global heart transplant shortage, accompanied by increasingly tense music. William Cohn, MD, appears in a museum setting, emphasizing the donor heart scarcity, while subtle mechanical whirring is audible. Archival footage shows a surgeon in a lab coat, as Dr. Cohn highlights the need for alternatives. A close-up of a complex mechanical device is shown, followed by a vintage animation of the circulatory system. The animation transitions to a detailed anatomical heart model as Dr. Cohn describes the heart's function, accompanied by suspenseful electronic music. The camera focuses on a museum display case showcasing a 'THORATEC' mechanical heart device. Cohn details Domingo Liotta and Michael DeBakey's early work, including DeBakey's experiments on cows. Archival footage shows Dr. Denton Cooley working on a mechanical heart component. Cohn narrates Cooley's implantation of the device in patient Haskell Karp, followed by vintage color footage of the surgical team operating on Karp, who is intubated and monitored, with a heart-lung machine visible. Cohn states this was 'the world's first clinical use of a total artificial heart' on April 4th, 1969, followed by a formal archival voice declaring its feasibility. Archival footage from April 1969 shows a news anchor reporting on Mr. Karp's regained organ function. The scene transitions to close-ups of medical components and a heart undergoing open-heart surgery. Dr. Cohn explains the early artificial heart's valve issues, leading to kidney failure and the patient's death 36 hours after an emergency transplant. Cohn, back in the museum, gestures beside an anatomical heart model, emphasizing the procedure's significance. Surgical close-ups show a mechanical valve being inserted and a spherical artificial heart component. Cohn details the device's bladders and one-way valves, concluding that the technology continued to evolve.

Surgical Team in Operating Room.description: A black-and-white archival clip showing a surgical team in an operating room, with a sign reading 'OPERATING ROOM 1', performing a procedure on a patient under drapes.

Heart-Lung Machine.description: A complex mechanical device with rotating parts and tubes, shown in color, likely a heart-lung machine used in open-heart surgery.

Mechanical Device (Tubes and Connectors).description: A detailed mechanical heart device with tubes and connectors, labeled 'THORATEC,' shown in a museum display. This device is central to the discussion by William Cohn, MD, who describes it as the 'first artificial heart' designed by Domingo Liotta and Michael DeBakey, initially implanted in seven cows for up to 12 hours.

Title Card.description: A black background with the text 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' and 'Presented By Lilly A MEDICINE COMPANY', surrounded by animated pills and capsules.

William Cohn, MD.description: A man in a blue blazer and white shirt, initially heard narrating over surgical footage with a clear, calm voice, explaining the failure of early artificial heart valves and a patient's tragic death. He is later seen seated in a museum-like environment with anatomical models, gesturing as he speaks, describing the mechanics of an artificial heart with 'bladders' and 'one-way valves' that used 'compressed air' to drive blood, and how this technology 'continued to evolve'.

Anatomical Heart Model.description: A detailed, colorful model of a human heart with visible arteries and chambers, mounted on a stand, shown in close-up against a dark background.

Man in Lab Coat.description: Archival black-and-white footage shows a man in a lab coat and glasses, identified as Dr. Denton Cooley, meticulously working on a mechanical heart component under a magnifying lamp. William Cohn, MD, states that Dr. Cooley, then partner to Michael DeBakey, implanted the artificial heart in a 'desperately ill human patient.'

Vintage Human Circulatory Animation.description: A stylized, vintage animation of a human body with a highlighted circulatory system, transitioning to a full-body silhouette with glowing points, symbolizing artificial integration.

Playmate Plus Cooler.description: A white and blue portable cooler labeled 'Playmate Plus', being carried by a person in scrubs, likely used for transporting medical equipment or organs, shown as the narrator details the global shortage of donor hearts.

Museum Display Case.description: A glass display case containing various medical artifacts, including anatomical models, located in a hallway that William Cohn walks through.

Patient in Operating Room.description: A man lying on an operating table, intubated and monitored, showing signs of distress during a surgical procedure. William Cohn, MD, identifies him as Haskell Karp, the 'desperately ill human patient' who received the world's first clinical total artificial heart on April 4th, 1969.

Artificial Heart Valve.description: A white, mechanical valve being inserted into a heart during surgery, shown in close-up with surgical tools.

Spherical Artificial Heart Component.description: A white, spherical device with a transparent center, likely part of an artificial heart, shown in a close-up view.

Medical Monitor.description: A circular screen on a medical device labeled with 'FOCUS', 'BRIGHTNESS', and 'SPEED', displaying a faint signal, likely for monitoring physiological data.

Archival News Anchor.description: A man in a white shirt and tie, speaking directly to the camera in a 1969 news film, with text overlay indicating the date and source. He speaks with a formal, slightly dated broadcast voice, reporting on 'Mr. Karp' regaining organ function, suggesting a 'mechanical heart' is a 'feasibility'.

raw_segments: The video opens with a title card displaying 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' presented by Lilly, surrounded by animated pills and capsules, as gentle, slightly melancholic background music begins. Archival footage then shows a black-and-white surgical team in an operating room, performing a procedure, while a male narrator explains that the only gold standard for heart failure is replacing the failing heart with a healthy donor. The scene transitions to a modern-day shot of a person in scrubs carrying a white 'Playmate Plus' cooler, followed by a black screen with animated graphics illustrating the severe global shortage of heart transplants: 6,000 performed annually versus 1,000,000 requested worldwide and 600,000 in the U.S., as the narrator details these statistics and the background music becomes slightly more tense. William Cohn, MD, a cardiothoracic surgeon at Texas Heart Institute, then appears in a museum-like setting with anatomical models, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, authoritative voice, explaining that the main limitation to transplantation is the availability of donor hearts. As he walks through a hallway, passing a glass display case containing medical artifacts, the narrator introduces him as an innovator in artificial heart technology, accompanied by a subtle mechanical whirring sound. Archival footage reappears, showing a surgeon in a lab coat working on a patient, while Dr. Cohn elaborates on the rarity of finding a suitable donor heart. A close-up of a complex mechanical device with tubes and connectors, likely part of a heart-lung machine, is then shown as Dr. Cohn emphasizes the critical need for alternatives to transplanted hearts. The segment concludes with a vintage animation of a human body with a highlighted circulatory system, transitioning to a full-body silhouette with glowing points, symbolizing the integration of artificial systems, as Dr. Cohn describes the heart as 'just a pump' that supplies nutrition and oxygen before the audio fades.

The segment opens with a vintage animation of a human body, highlighting the circulatory system with glowing points, transitioning to a detailed anatomical heart model. William Cohn, MD, a cardiothoracic surgeon, is then seen in a modern museum setting, speaking in a clear, informative voice about the heart's function, stating it's 'the hardest to pump that makes the blood move' and 'a very, very simple device by some metrics, but incredibly complex by others.' A subtle, slightly suspenseful electronic background music begins as he gestures toward a display case. The camera focuses on a museum display case showcasing a mechanical heart device with tubes and connectors, labeled 'THORATEC,' alongside informational plaques. Cohn continues, explaining that 'the really first artificial heart was designed by a guy named Domingo Liotta,' a 'brilliant Argentinian scientist' who worked with 'Michael DeBakey, pioneering heart surgeon in Houston.' He details how they 'developed a device that DeBakey had implanted in seven cows,' with the longest lasting 'about 12 hours.' Archival black-and-white footage follows, showing Dr. Denton Cooley, a man in a lab coat, meticulously working on a mechanical heart component under a magnifying lamp. Cohn narrates that while DeBakey was seeking funding, his partner 'Denton Cooley implanted it in a desperately ill human patient, Haskell Karp.' The video then cuts to a vintage color clip of a surgical team in an operating room, performing a procedure on patient Haskell Karp, who is shown lying on the operating table, intubated and monitored, with a mechanical heart device connected to his chest. A heart-lung machine and a graph recording vital signs are visible, and the patient's face shows signs of distress as medical staff monitor the procedure. Cohn concludes, stating this event on 'April 4th, 1969' was 'the world's first clinical use of a total artificial heart.' A different, more formal archival voice then declares, 'The possibility has now been established...', as the background music continues.

The segment opens with archival footage from April 1969, where a man in a white shirt and tie, identified by an overlaid text as 'Network News Film April 1969', speaks directly to the camera with a formal, slightly dated broadcast voice, reporting on a patient named 'Mr. Karp' whose regained organ function indicates a 'mechanical heart' is a 'feasibility.' The scene then transitions to close-ups of dark rectangular medical components, followed by a surgical view of a heart undergoing an open-heart procedure with tubes and clamps, and a medical monitor displaying a faint signal. Over these visuals, William Cohn, MD, narrates with a clear, calm voice, explaining that the early artificial heart's valves were 'breaking up the blood,' leading to kidney failure and, tragically, the patient's death from infection 36 hours after an emergency transplant, despite 64 hours on the device. Cohn then appears in a modern, museum-like setting, gesturing as he speaks beside a glass display case containing an anatomical heart model, emphasizing that 'it showed us that that type of thing was possible.' A brief, subtle background music plays before the video returns to surgical close-ups of a mechanical valve being inserted into a heart and a detailed view of a white, spherical artificial heart component. Cohn continues his interview, using hand gestures in a well-lit educational space, detailing how the heart had 'bladders that filled and emptied with compressed air that drove the blood forward,' using 'one-way valves' to manage blood flow, concluding that 'that device continued to evolve' based on this technology.

event_blocks.summary: A white mechanical heart device labeled 'SynCardia,' described by William Cohn, MD, as the 'world's first approved device' with improved membranes, valves, and manufacturing, is shown resting on a red surface. A woman in a red jacket and black pants walks through a hospital corridor with a backpack and cane, symbolizing independence. An anatomical heart model and a mechanical heart device are tested in a lab, with 'European Patent Office' text visible. William Cohn, MD speaks in a museum setting as the CARMAT device is announced as approved for commercial sale in Europe for 'temporary use.' A hand holds a white, spherical artificial heart component, followed by scenes of an ICU patient with medical staff and a surgical team performing a procedure. A hand connects a small black CARMAT device to a belt, illustrating its portable nature. A different male voice explains that SynCardia and CARMAT are the only approved artificial hearts, serving as 'bridge to transplant devices' until a donor heart is available, noting the longest an artificial heart has lasted is '1374 days, or roughly four years,' with 'long-term durability' being the main limitation. William Cohn, MD reappears briefly in the museum setting, and the segment concludes with a man attaching a black portable medical pouch to his belt, connected via a cable, demonstrating a wearable system, while a third male voice explains that a heart beating '80 times a minute' equates to '132,000 times in a day.'

Patient in Operating Room.description: A man lying on an operating table, intubated, with visible medical tubes and monitors, appearing distressed during a surgical procedure.

CARMAT Device.description: A small black device with a cable, labeled 'CARMAT', shown being connected to a belt, and described by a male voice as approved for commercial sale in Europe but only for 'temporary use,' and later identified by a second male voice as one of only two currently approved artificial hearts, used as a temporary 'bridge to transplant' device.

ICU Patient.description: A man in a hospital gown in an ICU room, connected to monitors, with medical staff attending to him, indicating post-operative or critical care.

Surgical Team in Operating Room.description: A black-and-white archival clip showing a surgical team in an operating room, with a sign reading 'OPERATING ROOM 1', performing a procedure on a patient under drapes.

Spherical Artificial Heart Component.description: A white, spherical device with a transparent center, likely part of an artificial heart, shown in a close-up view.

SynCardia.description: A white mechanical heart device with transparent chambers and tubing, labeled 'SynCardia', shown on a red surface, described by a male voice as the 'world's first approved device,' an evolution of earlier designs with improved membranes, valves, material, and manufacturing techniques, and later identified by a second male voice as one of only two currently approved artificial hearts, used as a temporary 'bridge to transplant' device.

Portable Medical Pouch.description: A black pouch attached to a belt, connected via a cable to a medical device, worn by a man in a black shirt, suggesting a wearable system for artificial heart support.

Museum Display Case.description: A glass display case containing various medical artifacts, including anatomical models, located in a hallway that William Cohn walks through.

European Patent Office.description: Text appearing in the top left corner during the lab scene, indicating the source or affiliation of the footage or setting.

William Cohn, MD.description: A man in a blue blazer and white shirt, seated in a museum-like environment with anatomical models, speaking and gesturing during an interview, whose voice (Male Voice 1) discusses the evolution and approval status of the SynCardia and CARMAT artificial heart devices.

Anatomical Heart Model.description: A detailed, colorful model of a human heart with visible arteries and chambers, mounted on a stand, shown in close-up against a dark background.

Woman with Backpack.description: A woman in a red jacket and black pants, smiling as she walks through a hospital corridor, carrying a backpack and a cane, symbolizing mobility and independence.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a white mechanical heart device, labeled 'SynCardia', resting on a red surface, as a male voice (likely William Cohn, MD) describes its evolution into the 'world's first approved device,' highlighting its improved membranes, valves, and manufacturing techniques compared to earlier implants. A smiling woman in a red jacket and black pants then walks through a hospital corridor with a backpack and cane, symbolizing independence. This is followed by a detailed anatomical heart model and a mechanical heart device being tested in a lab, with 'European Patent Office' text visible. William Cohn, MD is seen speaking in a museum-like setting as the male voice continues, announcing the CARMAT device's approval for commercial sale in Europe, though emphasizing its 'temporary use.' A hand holds a white, spherical artificial heart component, preceding scenes of an ICU patient with medical staff and a surgical team performing a procedure. A hand connects a small black CARMAT device to a belt, illustrating its portable nature. A different male voice then explains that SynCardia and CARMAT are the only approved artificial hearts, serving as 'bridge to transplant devices' to keep patients alive until a donor heart is available, noting the longest an artificial heart has lasted is '1374 days, or roughly four years,' with 'long-term durability' being the main limitation. William Cohn, MD reappears briefly in the museum setting, and the segment concludes with a man attaching a black portable medical pouch to his belt, connected via a cable, demonstrating a wearable system, while a third male voice explains that a heart beating '80 times a minute' equates to '132,000 times in a day.'

event_blocks.summary: William Cohn, MD, initially discusses the inherent challenge of creating artificial hearts that can withstand immense mechanical stress, a problem that has led BiVACOR, a startup with headquarters in Houston, Texas, and origins in Brisbane, Australia, to develop a radically different approach. Daniel Timms, a mechanical engineer and the inventor of the BiVACOR device, explains that their total artificial heart utilizes a magnetically levitated, spinning disk, eliminating physical contact between moving parts and thus offering potentially indefinite durability, a stark contrast to designs relying on compressing sacks. This impeller technology, building on earlier concepts from Rob Jarvik and Rich Wampler, is designed to pump blood to both the body and lungs simultaneously, with distinct settings for different pressure requirements. In a modern laboratory in Brisbane, Australia, the BiVACOR device and its gold-colored rotor are demonstrated within a complex fluid dynamics testing apparatus that simulates the human circulatory system, complete with transparent tubes labeled 'Pulmonary Artery' and '283mm Hg'. Initially operating in a continuous flow mode, the device is shown being transitioned to a pulsatile mode, where the rotor rapidly spins up and down to replicate a heartbeat, producing audible whirring and clicking sounds. This pulsatile function can be adjusted to mimic physiological changes, such as during exercise, showcasing the device's adaptability in a simulated environment, with a robotic arm assisting in the demonstration of catheter-like tube integration.

Black Case.description: A black, rectangular case placed on the lab table, likely containing equipment or tools related to the Bivacor device testing, visible as Daniel Timms works nearby.

Heart-Lung Machine.description: A complex mechanical device with rotating parts and tubes, shown in color, likely a heart-lung machine used in open-heart surgery.

Mechanical Device (Tubes and Connectors).description: A dimly lit, abstract mechanical apparatus with tubes and connectors, possibly part of an early medical machine, shown in a black-and-white archival clip, accompanied by a distinct mechanical whirring/humming sound.

Man in Casual Attire.description: A man in a blue shirt and jeans, holding a small, intricate mechanical heart model, standing in a well-lit, modern workspace.

Spherical Artificial Heart Component.description: A small, metallic, spherical device with a transparent center, held and examined closely by Daniel Timms, who identifies it as the BiVACOR device he invented.

Vintage Human Circulatory Animation.description: A stylized, vintage animation of a human body with a highlighted circulatory system, transitioning to a full-body silhouette with glowing points, symbolizing artificial integration.

Daniel Timms.description: A man in a blue shirt and jeans, actively demonstrating the Bivacor Rotor (Physical) and Bivacor Device (Physical), inserting the rotor into the device and observing the robotic arm’s interaction with the fluid dynamics testing apparatus. He speaks with a clear, explanatory tone, detailing the device's continuous flow and pulsatile modes, and its ability to adjust beat rates, while a distinct whirring and clicking sound accompanies the device's operation.

Brisbane, Australia Location.description: An outdoor urban setting with a building labeled 'Pathology Queensland' and 'Clinical Sciences', identified by a narrator as the location of BiVACOR, a startup headquartered in Houston, Texas.

William Cohn, MD.description: A man in a blue blazer and white shirt, seated in a museum-like environment, speaking thoughtfully and gesturing during an interview about the challenges of artificial hearts wearing out. His calm, articulate voice explains the difficulty of creating a device that 'never fails.' He is later identified by a narrator as Dr. Cohn, Chief Medical Officer for BiVACOR, and is seen observing equipment in a modern lab.

Anatomical Heart Model.description: A transparent, anatomical model of a human heart with visible chambers and vessels, placed on the lab bench, serving as a reference during the demonstration of the Bivacor device.

Laboratory Interior.description: A modern, well-lit lab with stainless steel tables, transparent fluid tubes, valves, pumps, and a black case, housing the Bivacor testing apparatus, with a sign reading 'DISCOVERIES PRODUCED HERE FOR THE COMMON GOOD' in the background.

Bivacor.description: A mechanical heart device, labeled 'Bivacor', is shown in a detailed 3D animation, highlighting its dual-chamber structure with blue and red fluid flows, a central rotating impeller, copper coils, green insulating layers, and a gold rotor. The first speaker (Daniel Timms) describes it as a 'rotary total artificial heart' with a 'spinning disk' that is 'magnetically levitated,' designed to pump blood to both the lungs and the body simultaneously, emphasizing its radical difference from compressing sacks and its potential to 'last forever.' Later, a narrator refers to it as 'Bivacor's artificial heart,' noting it works off 'rapidly spinning pump technology' and that 'Timms's device builds on that idea by using a spinning disk or impeller.' The device is later seen physically held by Daniel Timms.

Catheter-Like Tube.description: A white, flexible tube with a metallic tip, manipulated by the robotic arm to simulate connection to the Bivacor device within the fluid dynamics testing setup.

Bivacor Rotor (Physical).description: A gold-colored, multi-bladed rotor component with concentric grooves, held and rotated by Daniel Timms, then inserted into the Bivacor Device for demonstration. Its rapid spinning up and down is described as the mechanism for creating pulsatility, and its operation contributes to the audible whirring and clicking sounds of the device.

Bivacor Device (Physical).description: A metallic, dual-chambered mechanical heart with white tubing and visible internal components, shown being assembled and integrated into the fluid dynamics testing apparatus by Daniel Timms. It is described as capable of propelling blood in a continuous stream and later operating in a pulsatile mode, producing distinct whirring and clicking sounds as its internal rotor rapidly spins up and down to simulate a heartbeat, with the capability to adjust its beat rate.

Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus.description: A complex setup of transparent vertical tubes filled with liquid, connected to valves and pumps, labeled with handwritten text like 'Pulmonary Artery' and '283mm Hg', simulating a circulatory system for testing the Bivacor device.

Robotic Arm with Mechanical Heart.description: A white robotic arm with articulated joints, mounted on a metal frame, interacting with the fluid dynamics testing apparatus and manipulating the catheter-like tube to demonstrate device integration.

raw_segments: The video opens with William Cohn, MD, seated in a museum-like environment, speaking thoughtfully about the challenge of artificial hearts wearing out, noting that anything that has to flex "42 million times in a year" and "never fails" is a "tall order." His calm, articulate voice accompanies his gestures. The scene transitions to a black-and-white archival clip showing a dimly lit mechanical apparatus with tubes and connectors, possibly an early medical machine, accompanied by a distinct mechanical whirring and humming sound. The video returns to William Cohn in a close-up, with a colorful anatomical heart model visible in the background, as the mechanical sound fades. A narrator's voice then begins, explaining that "the durability factor has led other groups to rethink their approach." The setting shifts to Brisbane, Australia, where a man walks toward a building labeled 'Pathology Queensland' and 'Clinical Sciences,' carrying a black case, as the narrator introduces "BiVACOR, a startup from Brisbane, Australia, that's headquartered in Houston, Texas." Inside, he enters a modern laboratory where a complex mechanical device, resembling a heart-lung machine with tubes and a rotating component, is being operated. The narrator continues, stating BiVACOR "has come up with a device that hardly resembles a human heart at all" and has raised over $20 million, winning over "industry heavy-headers like Dr. Cohn, who serves as their chief medical officer." William Cohn is then seen walking through the lab, observing the equipment. The scene cuts to Daniel Timms, Chief Executive Officer & Chief Financial Officer of BiVACOR, seated in the lab, who introduces himself as "Daniel Timms," a mechanical engineer with a PhD in biomedical engineering. He states in a clear, informative voice that he "invented the BiVACOR device and have been developing that device ever since." He then holds a small, metallic, spherical artificial heart component, examining it closely. The video concludes with a 3D animation showing the artificial heart component being integrated into a human heart, highlighting its placement and function within the circulatory system.

The video opens with a detailed 3D animation of the 'Bivacor' mechanical heart device, showcasing its internal components as they disassemble and reassemble in a clean, white environment. The animation highlights the device’s dual-chamber structure with blue and red fluid flows representing blood circulation, and a central rotating impeller mechanism, zooming into copper coils, green insulating layers, and a gold-colored rotor. A male voice, identified as Daniel Timms, explains that "the artificial heart that we're creating is what's called a rotary total artificial heart," featuring a "spinning disk" that is "magnetically levitated" within a cavity to pump blood to both the lungs and the body simultaneously, similar to a fan but pumping blood. He emphasizes this approach is "radically different from utilizing compressing sacks," as the magnetically levitated spinning disk means "there's no touch down of any of those moving parts," allowing it to "effectively last forever." The animation returns, showing a top-down view of the device’s internal flow dynamics with red and blue particles. The video then cuts to real-world footage: a close-up of a white catheter-like tube being manipulated, followed by a gloved hand holding a small, metallic, turbine-like component. An animated diagram illustrates the device’s integration into a human heart. A narrator then states, "Bivacor's artificial heart works off a rapidly spinning pump technology," developed by scientists Rob Jarvik and Rich Wampler in the mid-1980s, whose device used "small turbines sat outside unhealthy hearts." The narrator concludes that "Timms's device builds on that idea by using a spinning disk or impeller," as Daniel Timms is shown holding the actual Bivacor device in one hand and its gold-colored rotor in the other, demonstrating the physical components in a well-lit, professional environment, with the on-screen text 'Bivacor' visible throughout.

The video opens with Daniel Timms, a man in a blue shirt, carefully holding and rotating a small, gold-colored, multi-bladed Bivacor Rotor, showcasing its intricate design and concentric grooves. He also holds the compact, metallic Bivacor Device, a dual-chambered mechanical heart with white tubing, demonstrating how the rotor fits into its central chamber. A male narrator explains that this 'impeller' is designed to push and regulate the flow of blood to both ventricles, describing it as a single disk with distinct veins on one side for pumping blood to the body and on the other for the lungs, tailored to different pressure requirements. The narrator further details the device's magnetic levitation system, which interfaces with two ring-shaped magnets, detecting the rotor's position and energizing coils to keep it levitated, thereby eliminating mechanical wear. The scene transitions to a modern, well-lit laboratory setting, revealing a complex fluid dynamics testing apparatus. This setup features transparent vertical tubes, labeled 'Pulmonary Artery' and '283mm Hg', filled with liquid and connected to a network of valves and pumps, simulating a circulatory system. A distinct mechanical whirring and pumping sound accompanies the visual of the Bivacor device integrated into the system, with the rotor spinning inside as fluid rhythmically flows through the tubes. The narrator confirms that 'Bivacor is currently testing its device in this system that circulates liquids to replicate the functions of the human body,' before raising a significant question about future human trials and Bivacor patients.

The video opens with a close-up of a complex fluid dynamics testing apparatus, featuring transparent vertical tubes filled with liquid, connected by valves and pumps, labeled with handwritten text like 'Pulmonary Artery' and '283mm Hg', simulating a circulatory system. A male voice, identified as Daniel Timms, explains that while a pulse is fundamental, the Bivacor device can propel blood in a continuous stream, which works well in the lab and animal trials, though human effects are still being explored. The camera pans across the setup, revealing a white robotic arm with articulated joints, mounted on a metal frame, interacting with the system. Daniel, in a blue shirt and jeans, is seen adjusting components on the lab bench, which includes a black case and a small anatomical heart model. He carefully handles a gold-colored, multi-bladed Bivacor Rotor (Physical) and inserts it into the Bivacor Device (Physical), a dual-chambered mechanical heart with white tubing. Daniel's voice continues, stating the device is currently in a 'continuous flow mode' but they aim to introduce 'pulsatility'. As he describes turning on pulsatility, a distinct whirring and clicking sound emerges from the device. The robotic arm then manipulates a catheter-like tube, demonstrating the integration of the device into the fluid system. Daniel explains that the device is now running in a 'pulsatile mode,' reproducing a beat once every second by rapidly spinning the internal disk (rotor) up and down, which is clearly audible. The lab environment is modern and well-lit, with stainless steel tables and a sign reading 'DISCOVERIES PRODUCED HERE FOR THE COMMON GOOD' visible. Daniel observes the robotic arm’s precise movements, occasionally adjusting settings, and further explains the device's ability to change the 'beat rate' to adapt to scenarios like a patient exercising, with the whirring and clicking sounds continuing throughout the demonstration of the Bivacor device's functionality in the simulated circulatory environment.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a tablet interface displaying real-time medical device data, including motor position and speed, as a hand adjusts settings while a voice mentions adjusting to '80 beats per minute now' and discusses lower resting and sleeping pulse rates. The scene shifts to a robotic arm manipulating a fluid dynamics testing apparatus, simulating blood flow through transparent tubes, with a hand adjusting a valve, as a narrator explains Bivacor's intention for automatic heart rate adjustments based on blood pressure changes during physical activity. A 3D animation then visualizes the Bivacor artificial heart within a human torso, detailing its rotor position control and internal fluid dynamics with red and blue blood flow, accompanied by ambient music. The video briefly shows a car driving through a suburban area and a focused driver before transitioning to a workshop environment where Daniel Timms, a man in a blue shirt, meticulously disassembles and inspects an early prototype of the Bivacor Device (Physical) on a wooden workbench. He identifies horseshoe magnets and a motor within the device and examines a gold-colored Bivacor Rotor (Physical) with concentrically grooved impeller designs, noting his father, a plumber adept with circulation systems, machined it on a mini lathe next to the TV. Timms then appears seated on a black leather couch in an interview setting, reflecting that while the device wasn't ready in time for his father, its development is motivated by the impact of heart failure on patients and their families, a situation he and his mother have experienced for 15 years, which catalyzed their passion to continue the project.

Bivacor.description: A mechanical heart device shown in 3D animation with dual chambers, copper coils, green insulating layers, and a gold rotor, demonstrating patented rotor position control with blue and red fluid flows. A narrator (Voice 2) explains Bivacor's intent for automatic heart rate adjustment based on blood pressure changes during physical activity.

Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus.description: A complex setup of transparent vertical tubes filled with liquid, connected to valves and pumps, simulating a circulatory system for testing the Bivacor device.

Man in Casual Attire.description: A man in a blue shirt and jeans, seen driving and then actively assembling the Bivacor Device in a workshop. He is the primary speaker (Voice 1), narrating his two-decade project, motivated by his father's heart failure, and the early development of the device as a prototype in a garage, with his father, a plumber, contributing to the circulation systems.

Catheter-Like Tube.description: A white, flexible tube with a metallic tip, manipulated by the robotic arm to simulate connection to the Bivacor device within the fluid dynamics testing setup.

Tablet Interface.description: A tablet displaying real-time data from the Bivacor device, including motor position, current, speed, power, and waveforms, with a hand adjusting settings. The data relates to pulse rates, which the Man in Casual Attire (Voice 1) discusses in the opening, mentioning '80 beats per minute,' 'resting,' and 'sleeping' pulse rates.

Workshop Environment.description: A well-lit workshop with a wooden workbench, pegboard wall with tools, and various mechanical components and tools scattered around, indicating a hands-on engineering space. The sounds of mechanical assembly are heard, and Daniel Timms explains that parts of the device were machined here, or by his dad on a "mini lathe" at home.

Bivacor Rotor (Physical).description: A gold-colored, multi-bladed rotor component with concentric grooves, held and rotated by Daniel Timms in the workshop, then inserted into the Bivacor Device for demonstration. Daniel refers to it in the context of the device's internal components, specifically mentioning "impeller designs" which this rotor likely embodies.

Nostalgic Photo.description: A vintage photograph of two men, one older with glasses and a beard, the other younger, both smiling and holding a long white object, possibly a prototype. This photo visually represents the 'early days' and the 'prototype' discussed by the Man in Casual Attire (Voice 1), who developed the device with his father, a plumber.

Bivacor Device (Physical).description: A metallic, dual-chambered mechanical heart with white tubing and visible internal components, shown being assembled and integrated into the fluid dynamics testing apparatus. Daniel Timms describes it as one of the "very first prototype iterations" and explains where the motor fits inside, and points out "impeller designs" within the pump.

Robotic Arm with Mechanical Heart.description: A white robotic arm with articulated joints, mounted on a metal frame, interacting with the fluid dynamics testing apparatus and manipulating the catheter-like tube.

Interview Setting.description: A modern indoor space with a black leather couch and a glass partition in the background, reflecting lab equipment, where Daniel Timms speaks directly to the camera in an explanatory or interview format. Here, he discusses the personal and broader societal impact of heart failure, and the motivation behind the device's development, revealing a 15-year personal connection to the issue.

Daniel Timms.description: A man in a blue shirt and jeans, actively demonstrating the Bivacor Rotor (Physical) and Bivacor Device (Physical) in a workshop, while speaking clearly and informatively about the device's prototype iterations, internal components (like impeller designs), and sharing a personal anecdote about his dad machining parts. Later, in an interview setting, he speaks directly to the camera, explaining the broader impact of heart failure on patients and families, and the personal motivation behind the project, revealing his connection to the issue for 15 years. His voice is calm and explanatory.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a close-up of a tablet interface displaying real-time data from a medical device, including motor position, current, speed, and power, with a hand adjusting settings. A male voice (Voice 1) discusses adjusting to '80 beats per minute now,' and mentions 'resting' and 'sleeping' pulse rates, noting that the pulse rate will be 'lower.' The scene transitions to a robotic arm interacting with a fluid dynamics testing apparatus, simulating blood flow through transparent tubes, as a hand adjusts a valve. A different male voice (Voice 2, a narrator) explains that Bivacor intends for these heart rate changes to be automatic, based on blood pressure changes during physical activity. A 3D animation then illustrates the Bivacor artificial heart inside a human torso, highlighting its patented rotor position control and internal fluid dynamics with red and blue blood flow, accompanied by gentle, ambient background music. The video cuts to a car driving through a suburban area, then to a man driving, focused on the road. This man (Voice 1) then appears in a workshop, assembling a metallic, dual-chambered Bivacor device with tubes and connectors on a wooden workbench. He narrates that this is a project he's worked on for 'almost two decades,' motivated by his father's heart failure during his undergraduate studies. He recalls that in the 'early days,' the goal was to 'make something a little naively to help his failing circulation,' and that they did 'some of the work in our garage or in our home.' A nostalgic photo of two men, one older and one younger, holding a long white object (a prototype) is shown, as Voice 1 reveals his father was a plumber, 'very adept with circulation systems,' and that they decided to 'make a prototype of this device.' The segment concludes with a top-down view of the disassembled device parts, including a gold rotor, valves, and tubing, on the workbench, emphasizing the hands-on engineering process.

The video opens with the distinct sounds of mechanical assembly as hands meticulously work on a component on a wooden workbench. Daniel Timms, a man in a blue shirt, then appears in a well-lit workshop, carefully disassembling and inspecting a beige, multi-port mechanical device, identified as the Bivacor Device (Physical). He explains, "So this is one of the very first prototype iterations of the Bivacor device," detailing how different horseshoe magnets and a motor fit inside. As he removes and examines a gold-colored, concentrically grooved Bivacor Rotor (Physical), he points out, "we can see one of the very first impeller designs," adding a personal touch by revealing it "was actually made by my dad, he machined this on a mini lathe... next to the TV so he could work on the mini lathe while watching TV." The scene transitions to Daniel seated on a black leather couch in a modern interview setting, where he continues to speak directly to the camera. He reflects on the device's purpose, stating, "Although we are now creating a device and obviously not in time for for him," referring to his dad, and emphasizes the broader impact: "there's many other people out with heart failure and, you know, not only does it touch the patient with the heart failure, but their immediate family which, you know, is something that my mum and myself have been dealing with now for now 15 years... So that really catalyzed our the motivation and the passion really to get..."

event_blocks.summary: Daniel Timms, in an interview setting, discussed the goal of getting the Bivacor device to patients, followed by a close-up of the Bivacor Device (Physical) within a Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, accompanied by mechanical whirring and pumping sounds. At a workstation, a man in a gray sweater and a man in a blue shirt collaborated on dual monitors as a narrator explained Bivacor's FDA progress, durability, and animal studies, with a projected 2022 human implant. A Bloomberg news article highlighted the European approval of the Carmat total artificial heart, noting its manufacturing for commercial sale and hopes for longer-lasting performance as a permanent replacement. A dark lab scene labeled 'European Patent Office' showed a CARMAT Device on a testing platform with a tablet interface displaying real-time data. The narrator then addressed the "big remaining downside": briefcase-sized power packs requiring permanent transdermal connections. The scene shifted back to the Bivacor Device, a 3D animation of Bivacor in a human torso, a SynCardia device, and a man in a blue shirt and boxing gloves training with a portable medical pouch. The narrator emphasized that advancements in battery life and wireless charging were crucial for patient freedom. In a separate segment, a white robotic arm precisely manipulated a Catheter-Like Tube within a Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, while Daniel Timms demonstrated the Bivacor Device (Physical) with a red anatomical heart model. William Cohn, MD, speaking over ambient, futuristic music, discussed the potential for healthy transplant recipients and contrasted the low number of implanted artificial hearts with the high number of heart failure deaths. Visually present in a museum-like setting with anatomical models, Cohn predicted that converging technical forces would lead to long-lasting artificial hearts without transdermal wires, ultimately reducing heart failure as a cause of death. The video concluded with end credits featuring the 'Lilly' logo and animated pills, followed by a teal 'O' logo, as the background music faded.

3D Animation of Bivacor in Human Torso.description: A 3D animation showing the Bivacor device integrated into a human torso, with dual chambers and copper coils, demonstrating its placement and function within the circulatory system, with a glowing effect highlighting the device's operation.

CARMAT Device.description: A small black device with a cable, labeled 'CARMAT', shown as a gold-colored, perforated component on a mechanical platform in a dark lab scene labeled 'European Patent Office'. A male narrator describes Carmat as a France-based company that received EU approval at the end of 2020 and has started manufacturing its hearts for commercial sale, with hopes for longer-lasting performance and becoming a permanent replacement.

Black Case.description: A black, rectangular case placed on the lab table, likely containing equipment or tools related to the Bivacor device testing, visible as Daniel Timms works nearby.

Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus.description: A complex setup of transparent vertical tubes filled with liquid, connected to valves and pumps, simulating a circulatory system for testing the Bivacor device, with a robotic arm manipulating a catheter-like tube.

Catheter-Like Tube.description: A white, flexible tube with a metallic tip, manipulated by the robotic arm to simulate connection to the Bivacor device within the fluid dynamics testing setup.

Anatomical Heart Model.description: A transparent, anatomical model of a human heart with visible chambers and vessels, placed on the lab bench, serving as a reference during the demonstration of the Bivacor device, held by Daniel Timms in the final shot.

Bloomberg News Article.description: A screenshot of a Bloomberg article titled 'European Regulators Approve Sales of First Artificial Heart,' dated December 24, 2020, with text highlighting Carmat's approval and stock performance, including a photo of a Carmat employee holding a beige, perforated artificial heart. The article's content is directly referenced by a male narrator, who details Carmat's EU approval and commercial manufacturing.

Bivacor.description: A mechanical heart device shown in 3D animation and physical form, whose company, Bivacor, is described by a male narrator as currently working with the FDA, undergoing durability and animal studies, and poised for its first human implant in 2022.

Interview Setting.description: A modern indoor space with a black leather couch and a glass partition in the background, reflecting lab equipment, where Daniel Timms speaks directly to the camera in an explanatory or interview format.

Portable Medical Pouch (SynCardia).description: A black pouch attached to a belt, connected via a cable to a medical device, worn by a man in a blue shirt and boxing gloves during a boxing training session. This visual illustrates a male narrator's point about the current 'briefcase-sized power packs' that limit an artificial heart patient's freedom.

Man in Gray Sweater.description: A man in a gray sweater, standing at a workstation with dual monitors, using a mouse to control or analyze data related to the Bivacor device, collaborating with another man in a blue shirt.

Tablet Interface.description: A tablet displaying real-time data from the Bivacor device, including motor position, current, speed, power, and waveforms, with a hand adjusting settings, and French labels such as 'Pression relative ventriculaire gauche (mmHg)'.

SynCardia.description: A white mechanical heart device with transparent chambers and tubing, labeled 'SynCardia', shown on a red surface next to a gray 'Freedom' device. It is visually linked to a male narrator's discussion about the need for advancements in battery life and wireless charging to improve artificial heart patients' freedom, as a man is shown training with a punching bag while wearing a portable medical pouch connected to a device, illustrating current limitations.

European Patent Office.description: Text appearing in the top left corner during a dark lab scene, indicating the setting where a CARMAT Device is being tested, while a male narrator discusses Carmat's aspirations for its artificial heart.

Man in Blue Shirt (Workstation).description: A man in a blue shirt, seated at the workstation, observing the dual monitors alongside the man in the gray sweater, likely involved in data analysis or device control.

Laboratory Interior.description: A modern, well-lit lab with stainless steel tables, transparent fluid tubes, valves, pumps, and a black case, housing the Bivacor testing apparatus, with a sign reading 'DISCOVERIES PRODUCED HERE FOR THE COMMON GOOD' in the background.

Bivacor Device (Physical).description: A metallic, dual-chambered mechanical heart with white tubing and visible internal components, shown being assembled and integrated into the Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, with a gold rotor and copper coils.

Robotic Arm with Mechanical Heart.description: A white robotic arm with articulated joints, mounted on a metal frame, interacting with the fluid dynamics testing apparatus and manipulating the catheter-like tube.

Title Card.description: A black background with the text 'prognosis <The Artificial Heart>' and 'Presented By Lilly A MEDICINE COMPANY', surrounded by animated pills and capsules.

William Cohn, MD.description: A man in a blue blazer and white shirt, seated in a museum-like environment with anatomical models, speaking animatedly and gesturing during an interview, whose voice is heard throughout the segment discussing the current state of heart transplants, the number of artificial heart implants versus heart failure deaths, and the future potential of long-lasting artificial hearts without external wires to eliminate heart failure as a leading cause of death.

Museum Display Case.description: A glass display case containing various medical artifacts, including anatomical models, located in a hallway that William Cohn walks through.

raw_segments: The video opens with Daniel Timms in an interview setting, seated on a black leather couch, concluding a thought about getting the device "into those patients that need it." The scene transitions to a close-up of the Bivacor Device (Physical) integrated into the Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus, where a distinct mechanical whirring and pumping sound begins, simulating a circulatory system. Two men, one in a gray sweater and another in a blue shirt, are then shown collaborating at a workstation with dual monitors. A male narrator's voice explains that Bivacor is "currently working with the FDA, undergoing durability and animal studies," and is "poised for their first human implant to take place in 2022." The footage then cuts to a Bloomberg news article highlighting the European approval of the first total artificial heart, featuring a Carmat employee. The narrator continues, stating that "France-based Carmat received approval from the EU at the end of 2020, and has started manufacturing its hearts for commercial sale," with hopes that "Carmat's heart will prove longer lasting than earlier artificial hearts" and become "a permanent replacement, not just a stopgap." This is followed by a dark lab scene labeled 'European Patent Office,' showing a CARMAT Device being tested on a mechanical platform, with a tablet interface displaying real-time data, including French labels. As a subtle, serious background music track begins, the narrator shifts to discussing a "big remaining downside": the "briefcase-sized power packs that have to stay permanently connected to the hearts through the patient's skin." The scene shifts back to the lab with the Bivacor Device, then to a 3D animation of the Bivacor device inside a human torso, followed by a SynCardia device on a red surface, and a man in a blue shirt and boxing gloves training with a punching bag, wearing a portable medical pouch connected to a device. The narrator emphasizes that "advancements like extending battery life and replacing cable drive lines with wireless charging technology would have to be realized before an artificial heart patient's freedom is truly..." as the audio fades. The final shot shows Daniel Timms in a dark blazer, holding the Bivacor Device (Physical) and a red anatomical heart model, demonstrating the device’s connection to the human heart, with the robotic arm and testing apparatus visible in the background.

The segment opens in a modern laboratory, where a white robotic arm with articulated joints, identified as the Robotic Arm with Mechanical Heart, precisely manipulates a Catheter-Like Tube within a Fluid Dynamics Testing Apparatus. Simultaneously, Daniel Timms, in a dark blazer, demonstrates the Bivacor Device (Physical) by holding a red anatomical heart model, illustrating its integration with the apparatus via white tubing. Throughout this, a male voice, later identified as William Cohn, MD, begins to speak over a light, ambient, and slightly futuristic background music, discussing the potential for a 'healthy transplant recipient.' The scene then transitions to a close-up of a computer monitor displaying real-time data from the Bivacor device, including graphs for 'Pressure Sensors' and 'Flow Sensors', as a Man in Gray Sweater uses a mouse to control or analyze the data, observed by a Man in Blue Shirt (Workstation). William Cohn's voice continues, noting that 'we're still in this low phase,' with '4 or 5 thousand pumps implanted a year,' contrasting this with '400,000 people dying of heart failure.' The setting shifts to a museum-like environment where William Cohn, MD, now visually present in a blue blazer, speaks animatedly and gestures, with a Museum Display Case containing anatomical models behind him. He elaborates on 'a whole bunch of technical forces that are converging on having an artificial heart that lasts a long time, that doesn't have a wire through the skin,' predicting that 'heart failure won't be the number one cause of death in the world.' The video concludes with end credits on a black background, featuring the 'Lilly' logo, animated pills and capsules, and production roles, followed by a teal 'O' logo, as the background music becomes more prominent before fading out.
